title: Clearance Equation and Classification
buzzwords: Volume of plasma scrubbed per unit time
Reported in milliliters per minute
Numerator is excretion rate
Denominator is plasma concentration
Below filtration suggests net reabsorption
Above filtration suggests net secretion
Compared against GFR to classify handling
Equals (Ux times V) divided by Px

title: Inulin Clearance
buzzwords: Exogenous polysaccharide given by infusion
Freely filtered across the glomerulus
Neither reabsorbed nor secreted by tubules
Metabolically inert and protein-free
Excretion equals filtered load exactly
Requires continuous infusion and timed collection
Impractical outside research settings
Gold standard for measuring true GFR

title: Creatinine Clearance
buzzwords: Endogenous waste from muscle metabolism
Produced at a relatively constant rate
Measurable from serum without infusion
Filtered and modestly secreted in proximal tubule
Overestimates true GFR by 10-20 percent
Most common clinical surrogate for filtration
Used to stage chronic kidney disease
Refined by MDRD and CKD-EPI equations

title: PAH Clearance and Renal Plasma Flow
buzzwords: Quantifies perfusion to the kidneys
Filtered and avidly secreted in proximal tubule
Handled by organic anion transporters
About 90 percent extracted in one pass
Yields the 'effective' qualifier on plasma flow
True flow equals measured value over extraction ratio
Para-aminohippuric acid is the classical tracer
Combined with hematocrit gives renal blood flow

title: Renal Blood Flow and Filtration Fraction
buzzwords: Approximately 20-25 percent of cardiac output
Roughly 1000-1200 mL per minute in adults
Plasma flow scaled by one minus hematocrit
Normal filtered fraction is 15-20 percent
Rises in heart failure via efferent constriction
Falls in pregnancy from disproportionate vasodilation
Angiotensin II preserves filtration during hypoperfusion
Ratio of GFR to renal plasma flow

title: Tubular Transport and Drug Clearance
buzzwords: Proximal tubule carriers move drugs against gradients
Competition between substrates lowers elimination
Saturation produces non-linear kinetics
Urine pH alters ionization and passive reabsorption
Dose reduction or interval extension corrects accumulation
Penicillins, methotrexate, and NSAIDs share an anion pathway
Metformin, cimetidine, and trimethoprim share a cation pathway
Mediated by OAT and OCT transporter families
